content: Founded for traditionalists from B'nai Aaron & Har Zion. Rabbi David Novoseller was guiding figure.
Held services in row house west of 60th St. In 1932/3 Shul was built on site of demolished home. In 1970's combined with Lenas Hazedek (59th & Larchwood).
Congregation met in a member's home. [Vilna]
Traditionalists who broke away from the West Oak Lane Jewish Community Center. Services led by Leo Landsman at his home.
Started as a Landsleit Shul. Verein founded for devotional purposes possibly the Podolier Assoc. $3/week membership required of all male members (up to age 40). Listed in 1901 at 610 S. 3rd.
Founded as the Jewish Community Group of Roxborough. In 1949 took over the Epiphany Lutheran Church, renamed Ivy Ridge Jewish Community Center. Moved to current site in 1965 and renamed to Or Ami.
Founded as the Tacony Hebrew Congregation. Changed to The Northeast Jewish Community Center in the early 30's. Chartered in 1937. A new Synagogue was built - Temple Menorah - in 1951.
Combined with B'nai Halberstam in 1893

Conservative group within the liberal reform congregation of Temple Zion in the Northeast. Broke off and held weekly services at the Medical Mission (near Pine & Strahle Sts.). Merged with Temple Menorah at Tyson & Algard in 1989.

At NE corner 31st & Page. Noted for charitable work and study sessions (Mishnayos) each night. Zionist organizers (Mizrachi) in 1930s. Three storey red brick building built in 1896, remodeled in 1915. Additional building added in 1921. Records held by Mr. Glassman (1918 N. 32nd St).
Also founded cemetery of same name.
Held services at 432 N. 2nd St until purchase of Marshall St. building in 1907
Founded by children of members at Beth Elohim (Woodland Ave.) Could be same as Beth Am Israel (Warrington).
Moved from Strawberry Mansion to Wynnefield in 1958.
One of several shtieblech (house shuls) in Wynnefield in the 1930's. Others included 5601 Lebanon Ave (home of the kosher butcher, Charles Zatz), and 58th & Malvern.
After combination in mid 80's known as Melrose B'nai Israel Emanue-El.
Founded as the Rumanian-American Synagogue until 1967. Charter hangs in the Shul.

First met in a Quaker meeting house on Gravel Hill Road; then at a store front on Street Rd.; Penn Valley Park on the Old Lincoln Highway, and then the Mcfadden Estate . The charismatic character of Rabbi Parker, the long standing rabbi, gave the Shul its nickname - Parker's Place.

Founded by the daughter of a Baptist pastor, Louise Daley, in the 1950's. Conservative, though institutionally independent. Claims 250 members but not currently listed as an active Philadelphia Shul. [African-American]
Formed when Har Tikvah, B'nai Ohr and B'nai Solomon combined in the 1970's . Beth Judah joined with Bustelton-Somerton in the early 1980's. Combined with Shaare Shamayim in the 1990's.
Began in private home at 6739 N.16th St. Architect Louis Kahn designed synagogue building in 1937
Moved from Kensington in the 70's. A short lived schtiebl that closed when Rabbi Isadore Solomon returned to Israel.
Architectural design by N. Bressi, 1966.

Started in the basements of ranch homes. Purchased site in 1964 at 7816 Halstead St. Committed to staying put despite of the shrinking size of the community in the 1960's. Built a larger sanctuary on Halstead St.

Chevra was Shul and Cheder. Services held above a crockery store, a pool room, and across from a box factory (1900-03) at Adams St. & Kensington Ave. 1903-5 moved to Front St. & Susquehanna Ave. and in 1905 to Boston Ave. where a home was remodeled as synagogue. Further remodeled in 1914. Records are held by Joseph Silverstein (2202 Frankford Ave) and Mildred Seidman (1604 N 2nd St).

Chartered as Southern Talmud Torah in 1904. Rented space at 727 Dickinson St. & 601 Tasker St until Dickinson St. Shul was built. Combined in 1930s with the Bessarbier Talmud Torah to form the Jewish Education Center #1. Records held by Sam Grabovoy (419 Dickenson St).
Services held in rented rooms until purchase of Schaunbracker Hall (S. 4th). Many members were craftsmen, & most notably carpenters.

Founded by Wolf Ettinger and Moses Blumenthal with services held at Horner's Hall, 3rd & Brown. Purchased land in Frankford for a burial ground. In 1865 acquired synagogue building at NewMarket & Noble Sts. from Keneseth Israel . Ten years later moved to the Juliana St. Synagogue & merged with Adas Israel. Built a synagogue in 1885 at 7th & Columbia. Dedicated larger building at Broad & Diamond in 1912; enlarged and rededicated in 1926. Present Synagogue in Elkins Park was dedicated in 1964. By the early 1880's, had Reform orientation, and by 1900 under rabbi Klein, became Conservative. [Central European & German- speaking]
Founded by Bernard Diamond. Used a mid-19th century African-American church on Holme Ave. Lasted until the building of the Pennypack Jewish Community Center in 1959.
First at 725 Lombard St. Moved to larger location at 418 Lombard St. - former Universal Church. Combined with Anshe Rodeph Zedek in 1893 to form, Congregation Kesher Israel.
Services held at rented home (155 Fairmount)
Held services in rented rooms at NE corner of 2nd & Vine Sts. from 1917 to 1924 then purchased N. 6th St. building.
[African American]

Started as a Landsleit Shul in rented rooms at 1214 S. 3rd St. (1902-8). Combined with B'nai Israel in 1902 to form Shari Zedek. Building purchased in 1909. Records held by Joseph Bornstein (809 S 3rd St) and Mrs. Benjamin Solomon (1714 S 2nd St). [Rozhishtch, Rozishtchov (NE of Lvov)]

Founded by Charles Kline, Joseph Weitz & Nathan Leidner as a Landsleit Shul. First met around 7th & South Sts. The Garrick Theatre ("Wheatley Dramatic Hall") was purchased remodeled and dedicated in 1891. Largest Hungarian congregation in Phl. Emunath Israel and Ohev Sholom combined in 1887 to form this large Hungarian congregation. [Austro-Hungary] [Hungary]
Served commercial district at 38th & Haverford.

Services originally held at home of Phillip Miller. By 1920's this minyan had grown substantially and was renamed B'nai Israel of Fairmount. By late 1930's became basis for Fairmount Jewish Community Center.
A stiebl Shul held at home of rabbi Freifelder. Disbanded when rabbi died in 1972.
Combined with Anshe Birz in 1890 to form, Poale Zedek-Anshe Birz.
Started as a Landsleit Shul. First met in the basement of Atereth Israel at 6th & Morris Sts until purchase of a building at 6th & Cross Sts. [Bukier]
Broke from Beth Judah in early 20's over egalitarian services. Met in private homes.
Affiliated with B'nai Jacob (1929 N.31st). Organized by Jack Zweig. Moved to Oxford Circle
An orthodox congregation for young people. First started in South Philadelphia. This was Strawberry Mansion branch.

Served Germantown & Berks business district. Operated Talmud Torah at Schuyler Hall, 6th & Diamond Followed Ashkenaz rites. 2 storey red brick built in 1915. Services first held at 2044 Germantown Ave. Records held by Irving Carroll (1923 N 8th St) & Mrs. Glouser (2103 N Franklin St).
Met at the home of the President & a founding member, Abe Liebowitz.

Started as a Landsleit Shul. Free Synagogue & Talmud Torah with community support. Part of Hebrew Free School movement. Set of homes remodeled as Synagogue & Talmud Torah in 1912. Demolished in the 1930's. [Bessarabia]
[Hungarian]
After 1985 combined and became Melrose Park B'nai Israel-Emanu-El

Founded by Sidney Zion. Met at member's homes: 2935 N. 22nd, 2953 N.23rd & 2923 N.22nd. In 1918 purchased a 3 storey building at 2801 N. 25th. Moved to final location in 1921. Records held by Sam Balter (6529 Limekiln Pike) and Rose Rodack (1939 Chew St).
Started as a beneficial society. Founders protested the Reform direction of Adath Jeshurun. 120 members listed in 1901.

Met in home of Rabbi Aaron Popack at 2117 Bleigh Ave on High Holidays and festivals. Also rented space at the local movie theater for "overflow services." When Popack died, the congregation disbanded.
Formed in '50's from combination of Young People's Shaare Israel, Young People of Beth Samuel, Young People of B'nai Moishe
Served the businesses at 52nd & Haverford in early 20's. By late 30's many had moved. House remodeled as synagogue in 1925.
Moved from Strawberry Mansion in the late 50's.
Began for charitable & devotional purposes at 528 Bainbridge by Simon Nager before moving south to Catherine St.
Served commercial strip at 63rd & Woodland

Located in 4th St. business district. Became the R' Hirshel Shul in 1924. Congregation chartered a new synagogue, Congregation Chevra Tehilim, at 715 S. 5th in 1925 in a converted three storey row home.
Started as a Landsleit Shul. Met in rented quarters near Richmond's Upholstery shop.

Name changed from Samuel Rosa Nathan Ohev Zedek to [Chevra] Ohev Zedek in 1897. Began in rented rooms at 312 New St and then at a hall on NW corner at 2nd & Fairmount Sts until purchase of church at 7th & Oxford Sts. Combined in 1958 with Mt. Airy Orthodox Congregation to become Congregation Ohev Zedek. Built new synagogue at Mount Pleasant & Woolston Aves. in 1959/60. In 1968 the congregation Aitz Chaim Zichron Yaackov of Strawberry Mansion combined with Ohev Zedek to form, Congregation Ohev Zedek Aitz Chaim Zichron Yaacov. In the 70's the Logan synagogue was sold, and the congregation combined again - with B'nai Yisroel Anshe Sfard of Logan - to become B'nai Israel - Ohev Zedek, and moved to the NE, at Castor & Solly Aves. [Origins reported as a Hungarian Landsmenshaften, though not according to initial charter. ]
"Chapel" opened in 1958

Members broke away from the Eastern Women's Talmud Torah. The EWTT leased premises to the new congregation until mortgage was paid. Place for local businessmen to say Kaddish. Records held by Kerpel Rosenfeld (148 Vine St) and Jean Schurr (146 Vine St).
256-54 E. Roosevelt Blvd used as a religious school & synagogue. New synagogue was built 1948-51. Combined with Beth Emeth of Oxford Circle in the early 1980's
Sexton lived upstairs in 1930's
Free education subsidized by local businessmen. Members also met for services.
First met at 1981 Upland Way as a Conservative congregation. Became Traditionalist in the late 40's. Failing by the early 70's. Combined with Temple Adath Israel in 1985.
Services in rented space at 59 N. Salford until 1922 when new synagogue was built. Merged in early 1960's with ??? at 60th & Larchwood.

Served businessmen of South St., west of Broad at 1706 South St. Moved to 704 S.19th St, then 21st & South, above a pool hall. Had Sunday School. Converted a three storey home in 1912. [Austro-Hungarian]
Services at home of founder, Mr. Witkin, 2159 Ridge Ave. Served Columbia Ave. business district. White brick Romanesque building dedicated in 1914. Records held by Moishe Shainson (2006 Ridge Ave).
Started as a mutual aid society; eventually formed its own congregation and acquired a building in 1940.
Met in private homes until purchase of three-storey red brick building on S. 3rd. Combined with Shari Zedek Anshe Reisicher in the late 1920's.
Services held in private homes until building purchased and remodeled in 1916. [Rumanian]
Formed by parents of Hebrew Sunday School Society.
Moved in 1974 to Northeast (Castor & Solly Aves.) B'nai Halberstam (6th & Green) combined with B'nai Israel in 1963 and again with Ohev Zedek (Mount Airy) in the late 1990's.

Members followed custom of "Rimplen" to raise funds for a new building. Rimplen gathered pledges by rotating the weekly Sabbath "Kiddush" among the members of the cong. Built at 54th & Wynnefield in 1926 and rebuilt after a fire in 1947. In the 1970's moved to current home.

Founded in the Pelham Club for young Jewish professionals. Current Shul was dedicated in 1954. Conservative transformed into ecumenical center under Rabbi Charry; he actively campaigned to stem suburban flight and foster inter-communal relations in Mount Airy.

Started as a Landsleit Shul. Rented rooms at 831 N. 8th until 1926 purchase of a 3 storey red brick building remodeled as Shul. Records held by Morris Levenson (1419 N 6th St). [Lubavitch, NE of Mohilev/ SE of Vitebsk]
Founded by Louis Levin & Mr. Kaplan in private homes (622 Morris St & elsewhere in vicinity) until 1914.
Formed from former members of Ahavath Israel of Oak Lane & N.16th St. Closed in 1970 and combined with Congregation Ner Zedek in the NE. [Austro-Hungarian]
Started as a Landsleit Shul.
Breakaway from Shaare Zedek to remain Orthodox. Building demolished.

First permanent home was a mansion retrofitted (by the previous congregation, Adath Israel) as a synagogue, at 410 Montgomery. A new structure was dedicated in June 1960. Renovated in 2006. Unlike many synagogues in the suburbs, had no precursor within Philadelphia. It was the first Reform Congregation on the Main Line.
Purchased synagogue building from Agudas Achim Neizner Shul. [Rumanian]

Originally, the Hebrew German Society Rodeph Shalom. Founded 1795. Incorporated as Rodeph Shalom in 1802. Rented space at Pear St & Margaretta St until establishing own synagogue at 1 Broad St (Moravian Alley). Moved in 1821 to Church Alley between 2nd & 3rd Sts, then to 15 Vine St, Cherry St above 5th Sts, and 4th above Vine & Wood. In 1847 moved into a Church on Juliana between 4th & 5th. In 1870 dedicated own synagogue building at Broad & Mount Vernon Sts. In 1928 moved to the current location. [Central European; German speaking]
Named after founder, Jacob Israel Bernstein. Building purchased from Adath Jeshurun.
Moved from Overbrook Park to Main Line in 2007.

Founded by Samson, Dworkin & Litman. Services held in homes until synagogue built in 1929 on site of demolished home. Subsequently, the Millenium Baptist Church. Records held by Jacob Weiss (5262 Parkside Ave).
Northeast Philadelphia's first ReformTemple
Overseen by Rabbi Moses Varhaftig
Minyan held in store belonging to Oscar Perry. Perry had moved in the late 1920's from the Southern New Jersey agricultural settlements to open a sewing machine store in the city.

Combination of Chesed Shel Emeth & B'nai Moshe Gomel met first at 515 S. 9th St., then at 203 Pine St. and finally at 314 Catherine St. The building was given to the Central Talmud Torah as a free Hebrew school in 1903. [Gomel]

Services held in private homes until building purchased in 1923. Founded by Richard Eskowitz and David L. Pransky with Orthodox mission. First rabbi engaged in 1927. 2 storey buff stone corner row house. Interior remodeled as synagogue in 1922/23. Ladies' Balcony. Social Hall in basement. Old records, some in Yiddish, are held by Max Hodes (5804 Hazel St.) Subsequently, Mount Calvary Holy Church.

Met under aegis of Keneseth Israel. Formally organized in 1942 as Congregation Tikvah Chadoshoh. Met at Keneseth Israel & at 441 E. Loudon St. until purchase of chapel belonging to the old German Jewish Orphanage on Chew Ave. in 1951. Affiliated with the Conservative movement in the 1950's. Congregation disbanded in 1985, some joined the Oxford Circle Jewish Community Center & others Brith Israel of Feltonville. [German]
Started as a Landsleit Shul. [Podolia]
[Winitzer]
Services in private homes until purchase of synagogue building. Grey brick row home with new façade, 1927

Services first held at home, 449 S. 60th St. Cong built 2 story red brick Shul in 1919. Moved to Wynnefield in late 60's. Records held by Louis Levy (5834 Pine St) and Esther Freedman (130 S 53rd St).

Members previously belonged to Cong B'nai Menasha ("The White Shul") on N. 31st and Cong Chevra Tehillim on W. Cumberland. Combined with Zichron Jacob Talmud Torah in 1924. Romanesque building erected in 1923; Expanded 1932. Records held by Isaac Wiknan (2509 N 32nd St), Nathan Hankin (2536 N 30th St), Hannah Goldstein (1500 Firth St), and Israel Weiss (2533 N Hollywood St). Subsequently, Faith Temple.

One of 4 congs established in SW Philadelphia's farming communities - known as, "The Meadows." Many of the families had ties to the Jersey agricultural colonies. Combined with local Talmud Torah, circa 1914.
Orthodox group broke from Beth Emeth in 1954. Purchased row house on Large St. Membership had severely dwindled by the early 1980's.
Building dates to 1912
Formed from combination of Anshe Birz and Poale Zedek in 1890

Began as a beneficial association to distribute money to the Jewish poor of Palestine (through the Austrian Society of Jerusalem). Its founding President, A. Unterberger was previously active in the Hungarian Shul at 5th and Gaskill, but left to found own cong of Austrian Jews [Austrian]
Rabbi Meyer Isaacson moved congregation from South Philadelphia to the Northwest. The rabbi moved to Staten Island in the 1970's.
Store front Shul. Former members of Ziknai Israel at 5th & Christian Sts, and of Shomre Shabbas at 5th & Jackson Sts. Broke away from latter over procedural argument.
Started as a Landsleit Shul of the Kreminitzer Rebbe, a Hasidic congregation meeting in the Rebbe's home. The Shul closed when the Rebbe died. [Kremenitz, NE of Lvov]
Combined with Suburban Jewish Community Center-B'nai Aaron in 1991
Started as a Landsleit Shul of craftsmen. Rebuilt a colonial row house to serve as Shul.
Other locations: 512 N. Marshall St (1911) & 534 N.7th (1919)
Founded in 1907. Moved to its current location in 1961 and has merged with Beth Judah, Rodeph Zedek, and Beth Chaim.

Founded by Reuben Manebsky. Met at member homes - 730 Passyunk Ave & 240 Monroe St - until use of its own synagogue at 620 Minister St. in 1888. This building was sold in the 90's to Anshe Zitomer. New Synagogue built at 6th & Kater built in 1904. Roman Baroque & East European elements. Subsequently The Antiquarian Delight, an indoor antiques market. Home to the Tolner Rebbe in 1910's. Philadelphia's Hasidim, according to M. Freeman, were generally followers of the Berditchev and Pizhiner Rebbes. [1901 lists a Barditchiefner [Berditchev] Verein. [Members from Berditch, Pizhin and Tolener.]
Storefront synagogue. Moved to the 500 Block (NW corner of Rorer & Wyoming Ave) in the early 1950's. Building dedicated in 1954.

Started as a Landsleit Shul founded by Michael Asch. Chartered as The Krakauer Beth Elohim Beneficial Society in 1882 Rented rooms for services at 510 S. 5th St. until purchase of Shul. By 1947 no longer viable and changed its name to The Krakauer Beneficial Society. The Society celebrated its centennial in 1976. Early records of the synagogue/ society were kept in German. Krakauer combined with Beth Elohim in 1882. [Krakow, Poland]
"Wynnefield's Reform Temple" First pro-Zionist reform congregation in Philadelphia. In 1980's moved to Gladwyne, PA.
For early history see, Beth Tikvah. Purchased a building on Welsh Rd near Dewees St., then expanded to a store at Welsh Rd & Roosevelt Boulevard. New synagogue built in the 1960's.
Services in private chapel of Burd's home. Burd was also affiliated with Ohel Jacob
Held High Holiday services at Quaker City String Band Hall. A. Belier was early benefactor.
Previously at 843 S.3rd.
Served Tioga commercial district. Large row house synagogue.
Started in rented space at 203 Pine St, then 515 S. 9th St.
Beth Abraham merged into Beth Ami in the 1960's.
At 2nd & Vine Sts, 508 N. 2nd St & 926 New Market St. Moved to Logan (10th & Rockland) from 6th & Green in 1962. Moved to Northeast in 1978.
Founded in the Church of the Loving Shepard. Moved to current location in early 2000's.
Served commercial district at Torresdale & Longshore. Began with rented space in the Tacony Fire Hall, then the Tacony Trust Building.

Most of congregation were shopkeepers on Cheltenham and Germantown Aves. By WWII seating mixed, and much of service in English. Children of founders tended to prefer the Germantown Jewish Center. Combined with B'nai Jeshurun in the '50's. Subsequently used by the Zion Baptist Educational Center.
Zionists. Started in social hall. Drew a city-wide membership to its lectures on Zionism.
Started as a Landsleit Shul. High Holiday services held at Garrett Hall, 203 Pine St. [Hungary]
Named after principal benefactor of building fund, Samuel Israel. Three storey red brick building erected in 1922. Records held by Ida Sacks (418 Wolf St).
Broke from the Shomre Shabbas at 414 Christian St. Met on S. 5th St. until 1904; then at 324 Montrose St., the home of founding member, Julius Moskowitz.

Held services in private homes then in rented store (NW corner of 6th & Snyder) until 1912 construction of own Shul in a combined 2 red brick row homes. Records held by Benjamin Rosenfield (1914 S 6th St).

First met in rented space at 315 Catherine St. until moving to the SE corner of 5th & Dickinson Sts in 1910. Combined with the Kolker Synagogue (Raim Ahuvim Anshe Kolker) after WWII. Met at 5th & Dudley Sts. Building dates from 1927.
[Lubin]
Origins as a beneficial society (Greater Rumanian brotherhood) with daily minyan. Rented hall for High Holidays until congregation acquired new building. Joined by Or Chudosh in 1892. [Rumanian]
Served businessmen at 60th & Girard.
Functioned without rabbi. Had an active Ladies' Auxiliary. In 1950's moved to Wynnefield & N.E. After WWII supported by Kevy Kaiserman and Fredric Mann. Remodeled tan brick house; bimah in center.
Moved into 324 Catherine St. in 1919. [Vohlynia, White Russia]
A break away from Beth Judah meeting in private homes. Rented Tankin's Hall at 4708 N. Broad St. for High Holidays. Purchased church in 1955. After 1982 combined with Beth Tefilah of Pennypack Creek

Cantor Bernie Diamond left Pennypack Community Center for this congregation in 1961. He objected to Pennypack's social, rather than religious focus. Morrell Park cong was chartered as Beth Torah. Built new synagogue in 1966. Sold in 1978.
At intersection of Cambridge, Poplar & 42nd St.
Founded by rabbi Novitsky, Harry Foreman & David Marsel.
First held services at Caledonian Hall, 214 Pine St., and on the High Holy Days at Maennerchor Hall, Fairmount Ave. Cited by Morais Bernheimer. Beneficial purposes.

Founded by Orthodox merchants who conducted services in Hebrew and Yiddish. In 1925 converted a house at 206 N. Church St into a stone Shul. Became Conservative and in 1988 built new Shul in the European Ashkenazic tradition at current location.
Diminished, after David Wachtfogel's son immigrated to Israel in early 80's.
Ner Talmid merged with Beth El Suburban.
[Sephardic]

Began in Oak Lane above a five & dime on Ogontz, near 69th Ave. Leased a building at 6827 York Road in 1933 until building and dedication of synagogue on N. Broad in 1939. Chartered as The Liberal Congregation Temple Judea. Reform orientation. Rabbi Meir Lasker was an early Reform Zionist sympathizer. Combined with Keneseth Israel in 1982
Founded to serve the gay and lesbian Jewish community. First at 8 Leticia St in Old City, in October 2006 moved to Rodeph Shalom
Affiliated with the Jewish Education Center, a Labor Zionist org. Two branches of the JEC: 5th & Moore Sts and Marshall & Porter Sts. Herman Shlansky was an organizer who conducted services.

Before 1916 rented space at 625 S. 3rd St. & at 420 Catherine St. Devotional branch of the mutual aid society, the Independent Fastover Organization. Combined with Magidei Tehilim in 1960's, then with Shivtei Yeshurun in 1970's. Three storey brown & tan brick building; subsequently, The Prophetic Church of Christ. Records held by Jacob Goldstein (5859 Christian St) and Clara Kremmer (1642 S Orkney St). [Fastov, SW of Kiev]
Started as a Landsleit Shul. Affiliate of Anshe Sode Loven Beneficial Association. [Belaya Tserkov, S. of Kiev]
Free congregation. Organized by Ben Markowitz, a tailor and met in a hall for the High Holidays.
Organized by local merchants. Met at 964 E. Russell St. In 1937 added three- storey building, 3339 Jasper St., as community center.

Started as a Landsleit Shul. Met at home of Rabbi Grossman (503 Tasker) until 1930. "A true South Philadelphia house Shul," possibly Hasidic. Changed name to Ohavas Achim Ostreicher in 1930 and moved to 1523 S.5 St. [Galicia]

Rented space at 703 Master St. until purchase of building in 1922. Before charter known as Congregation Agudath Achim Machzikei Harov. 3 storey red brick home. Decorative transom with Magen David motif. Records held by Isadore Greenbaum (1447 N 8th St).
Served businesses on South St. Combined with Poale Zedek in late 1890's.
Non-payment of taxes closed this synagogue during the Depression.
Former members of Emanu-El who wanted to keep a neighborhood synagogue in Oak Lane. Rented quarters at 5604 N.13th St. Combined with B'nai Joseph to build a synagogue at 5701 N.13th St.
Chartered as a beneficial association. Moved to Poplar & Fairmount (at 5th). [Hungarian/Czech]
Some of founders came from Beth Israel.
Tolner Rebbe's Shul. Moved to Logan in the mid-1940's. Met at 4900 N. 8th St (at Rockland). Renamed the Tolner Synagogue. [Tolnoye, Kiev Province]
Center City Havurah Minyan members started this Shul. While no written records have been found, the Seltzer Chapel (room 100) has over 1,000 Yahrzeit plaques.
Combined with the Khelmer Shul after World War I.
Combined with Poale Zedek in 1900 to form, Poale Zedek-Sharith Israel. [Pruchauer ]
Moved from red brick 3-storey building to 205 Vine St in 1933, then to 820 N.5th St. Founders were women.
House Synagogue for members of the Landsmenshaften

Formed when Temple Brith Kodesh & Boulevard Park Congregation combined. Purchased a house on the corner of Wellington & Friendship Sts and became Ner Zedek. In late 60's Ezrath Israel at 69th & Ogontz combined with Ner Zedek. A new synagogue was built in 1969. In 1994, Beth Uziel joined the merger.
Started as a Landsleit Shul
Founded in SW Phl. Moved to Narberth then built current Shul in 1973.

Founded in Coatesville as Kesher Israel, probably as a house Shul. In 1907 purchased a cemetery. In 1916 name was changed to Beth Israel. In 1923 built a new Shul and enlarged it after WWII. Opened current site in 1995. [Eastern European]
Merger of Conservative congregations, Tiferes Israel of Norristown and Beth Israel of Lansdale.
First met at 4848 N. Front St.; Became Conservative in 1923. Synagogue built by end of 1920's. Combined with the Oxford Circle Jewish Community Center in 1990.
B'nai Jacob & Anshe Rodeph Zedek combined to form Kesher Israel in 1894. Now located in a former church rebuilt in Moorish revivalist style. See history written by Harry Boonin.
Founded by 20 members in a house in Havertown. In 1953 purchased current site and broke ground. In 1959 dedicated new sanctuary.
Founded by Yaacob Layzer Rustev. Started as a beneficial society. Continued to meet at Boslover Hall, 6700 Bustleton Ave into the 1990's. Combined with Lenas HaZedek in 1900. [Prushzvier, Shershow]
Established in the early 1890's near 3rd & Bainbridge Sts. Moved to Cedar St. in 1929 then to Wynnefield in 1958/1961. [Austro-Hungarian]
First held services in a disused church at New Market above Poplar. Dedicated in 1872.
Combined after First World War with Beth Hamedrash HaGadol.

Originally organized by members of the Max Bernstein Northern Free Loan Association. Building was owned by the mutual aid society. Services first held in rooms at 914 N. Franklin St. Records held by Max Newman (1447 N 6th St).
Splinter congregation from Emunas Israel-Ohev Sholom after latter moved into the "Garrick." Rejoined Emunas Israel in 1901/2.
Aitz Chaim-Zichron Jacob (Strawberry Mansion) moved to Mt. Airy & combined with Mt. Airy Orthodox, as did Ohev Zedek ( 7th & Oxford) in 1968
Used Masonic Hall for shopkeepers along Main St. Rev Fleischer was hired as a general functionary (shochet, baal koreh etc.) By WWII most of the Jewish population had moved away. [Hungarian & Polish]
See Adath Shalom

Unclear if the 3 Brith Sholom congs are the same. Various locations for identical name: one congregation held services at a hall, the SW corner of 6th & Girard in 1891; a Brith Sholom also started out on 4th St. below Poplar St, later moving to NE Corner of 2nd & Laurel. Reported to have been Molly Picon's Shul. [Hungarian]
Met at own homes in vicinity of 52nd & Overbrook in the 1950's.
Moved to N. 6th St in 1930's. Congregation led by Moshe Lipschitz (known as "The Philadelphier Rebbe"), closed in 50's when Lipschitz went to Israel.

Served Marshall St. business district on the same street as its members' businesses. First held services in rented rooms at SE corner of 7th & Master until purchase of synagogue in 1911. In the 1960's moved to the Northeast (2101 Friendship St. at Bustleton Ave). [1912]
Former members of Ziknai Israel (5th & Christian) who had moved to the vicinity of 5th & Jackson
D'rshe Tov founded in 1891 at 924-6 New Market St. in Northern Liberties. B'nai Jacob was founded in 1913 in Strawberry Mansion at 1927-9 N 31st St. Merged in 1962 and moved to current address.
Established by Solomon Isaacson (Meyer Isaacson's son) in Philmont Heights in the NE. First met in rabbi's basement near Bustleton & Tomlinson Roads.
Started as a Landsleit Shul. In 1920's & 1930's met at 730 Poplar St, 930 Franklin St & 1424 N.7th St. [Hungarian]

First held services at private homes, rented halls for High Holidays - including 322 Bainbridge St. Purchased the "Odd Fellows Hall", 756 S. 3rd in early1890's, sold in 1905 to the Poras Joseph Congregation. In S. 3rd in early1890's, sold in 1905 to the Poras Joseph Congregation. In 1905/6 purchased a disused church on S. 2nd St. Ladies' Auxiliary organized in 1930. Currently, Neziner has combined with Beth Zion-Beth Israel. [Nezhin, NE of Kiev & SE of Chernigov]

Started as a Landsleit Shul in rented rooms at 322 Christian St (1920-21), then on NE Corner of 4th & Segal (1922-25). Building purchased in 1925. Combined with Mishkan Israel. [Kolk (Kolki, S of Pinsk)]
Founded by Dorothy Greenstein to establish Reform Judaism in South Philadelphia. Supported by Keneseth Israel & Rodeph Shalom.
The Rebbe sold his house to Rabbi Ephraim Yolles of Kerem Israel (Strawberry Mansion) in the 50's and moved to Israel.

Held services at private homes - 2119 N.16th St. & 2126 N.16th St, latter remodeled as synagogue. Dedicated in '28. Served business district at 16th & Susquehanna. Records held by Sam Lehman (2119 N 16th St) and Ida Waldman (2141 N 15th St). Combined with Ezrath Israel.
Started in a row house in Mount Airy. In 1955 purchased and converted a mansion. In 1974 moved to Spring House and in 2006 to its current location.
Started as a Landsleit Shul. Synagogue building purchased by member, Max Goldberg, in 1921. No longer standing. [Vitebsk]
White stone building remodeled as synagogue, 1937. Records are held by Gedalia Zucker (1437 N. Franklin St) and Lottie Edelman (1410 N Seventh St).
One of two Hungarian congregations in vicinity.
Modeled after the Germantown Jewish Center. Flourished in the 1950's.

Keneseth Israel and Temple Gesellschaft combined in 1856 to form the Reform Congregation Keneseth Israel. Located at 528 N. 2nd St. from (1847-8); at New Market & Noble St. (1848-63). Built & dedicated own synagogue building at Sixth & Brown (1864-92). Dedicated a new building at Broad above Columbia Ave in 1892. In 1950 moved to Elkins Park.

Rented rooms in Washington Ave & Catherine St. area until moving south to a two storey red brick building dedicated in 1910. In 1960's paid members to attend services. Mixed seating by 1940's. [Brohin & Cholmetch, N of Kiev]
First met in 1947 in an empty home near Ridge & Green Lanes. Became Reform in the 1950's, moved further into suburban Roxborough and renamed Or Ami.

Reform oriented group from the Northeast Jewish Community Center. First met at the Odd Fellows Hall at Rhawn & Frankford. Chartered as Boulevard Temple, in 1950. First synagogue built in NE with off street parking. Moved to Verree on Welsh Rds in 1963 and renamed, Temple Beth Torah.
A break-away congregation from Rodeph Shalom. Practiced a conservative version of Reform. Followed rite formulated by Marcus Jastrow, Benjamin Szold and H. Hocheimer (known as Avodath Israel).
B'nai Zion was within a maternity home for young immigrant women run by the Rebecca Gratz Club.
Served businessmen of 5th & Lehigh. Began at 5th & Cambria. 2 storey red brick. Built 1875. Synagogue dedicated in 1916. Rebuilt 1953. Subsequently, Providence Baptist Church. [Austrian & Russian]
Started as a Landsleit Shul. Met at 3rd & Brown, at 481 N. Brown & at 630 N.2nd St. [Galician]
Records held by Rabbi Chairman (925 N Franklin St).
Started as a Landsleit Shul. First met in Silverman's department store on the corner of 6th & South. Combined with Poale Zedek in 1890 to form, Poale Zedek-Anshe Birz and met at 322 Bainbridge St.
Combination of Beth Judah (1905) and West Phl Hebrew School (1914). Absorbed by Beth Hillel in Wynnewood. Synagogue constructed in 1927-8.
Started in tailor shop on Mill St, Bristol. In 1935 purchased building on 100 block of Pond St. Moved to current site after WWII.
Formed when the Krakauer-Beth Elohim congregation moved.
Origins in Strawberry Mansion & Wynnefield

First Conservative synagogue in Philadelphia. Original location on Adelphia (between Walnut & Spruce) on 6th St., 1840-1849; then Crown St. (between 4th & 5th) above Race until 1894; moved uptown in 1890's to 8th & Jefferson; and further north in 1900's to 32nd & Montgomery site. Combined with Beth Zion (Center City) in 1964.
Founded in a converted greenhouse on the Clothier Estate. In 1960 joined by the West Philadelphia Jewish Center. In 1967 dedicated new building and in 1970 merged with Beth El.
Previous location at 916 S. 5th St.

Purchased church at 31st & Diamond Sts., and converted it in 1916. Dedicated new building at 33rd & Parkway Sts (between Diamond & Susquehanna Sts) in 1925. 33rd St building also used as a Community Center. Moved to Erdenheim, PA and renamed, Beth Tikvah-B'nai Jeshurun. Combined in 1957 with Adath Shalom (Mount Airy). Moved to former Dutch Reformed Church at Stenton Ave. & Dorset St. & changed name to B'nai Jeshurun of Mount Airy. Rust colored stone building, Byzantine & Romanesque elements, dedicated 1925.

Started as a Landsleit Shul. Originally chartered as Congregation Tiferes Israel Anshe Zitomer-Gubernia Wohlin Nusach Sfard. Used synagogue formerly owned by B'nai Reuben. Became Anshe Zitomer Nusach Sfard in 1903 on moving to Dickinson St. Moved to 2300 S. 6th St in late 1960's. [Zhitomir]
Founded when Kerem Israel & Ephraim Yolles moved to Wynnefield. Lasted until the late 1960's then moved to Rhawnhurst at 7926 Algona Ave.

Founded in Trenton, NJ as Hachaino B'nai Israelites, an Orthodox Shul. Moved to several locations in Trenton, became Conservative and changed name to Brothers of Israel. In 1970 branches started in Lawrenceville and Yardley. Changing Trenton demographics caused move to Yardley in 2007.
[Shavel, Poland]
See Adath Shalom
Organized primarily as a beneficial association for members by Mr. Kendall.

Started as a Landsleit Shul. Before purchase of synagogue in 1939 held services at 506 Wharton St.(1907-10); 503 Tasker St.(1910-30); 1523 S. 5th St(1930-38). On purchase of synagogue became Congregation Ohr Achaim. [Galician]

First met in hall at 39th & Poplar Sts., then in private homes until purchase of Girard Ave. building. Buff brick (formerly Parkside Republican Club) redesigned in 1923 and in 1934 after fire damage. Subsequently, First Timothy Baptist Church. [Austro-Hungarian & Lithuanian]
Property divided among 7 "directors" according to articles of constitution.
Daily minyan conducted at the home of Rabbi Album. Dedicated in 1943 as B'nai Zwi.
Served businesses around 5th & Olney. In late 50's moved to 2nd & Cheltenham.

Started as a Landsleit Shul at 728 Bainbridge St, 5th & South Sts & 514 Pine St until purchasing location on Lombard. One of first large Eastern European synagogues. Yarhzeit plaques some over 100 years old. Byzantine revival style. Façade completed in 1897. Interior modeled after the Bevis Marks Synagogue, London. Additions made in 1904 and 1909. [Russian]

Chevra Thillum Anshe Sfard became Zichron Jacob Talmud Torah in 1919. Combined with Aitz Chaim to form Aitz Chaim Zichron Yaacov in 1925. Yellow brick building built 1920; subsequently, Ebenezer Church of Christ.
Started as a Landsleit Shul. Combined with Kesher Israel in 1894. [Anshe Szager]
Started as a Landsleit Shul.

Started by women as a Sunday School. Parents formed congregation & in 1910 acquired a church building at 57th & Haverford Ave. Conservative congregation who suffered from a conflict with the local Orthodox congregation, but revived with building of a new synagogue at 58th & Walnut (funded by a Philadelphia businessman, Edward Rothschild, & dedicated in his parents' memory). 1915 structure contained a school. A chapel (in memory of Rothschild's two wives), a library and community center were added in 1922. Remodeled several years later and dedicated, in memoriam, to Edward Rothschild. In 1927 Beth-El contained a school, a sisterhood, a men's club, an Auxiliary, and more. Combined with Beth Hillel Wynnewood.
[African American]
See Adath Shalom
Organized as a beneficial society
[Talfer (Kiev, Lithuania)]
Mansion purchased for community by a benefactor - Tuvia - after whom the synagogue was named. Led by the Novoseller family.
Founded with help from Beth El at 58th & Walnut.
First written records from 1924. Moved from Frankford (home of rabbi Kramer at 4334 Paul St). Moved to current location in 1955. Closed 11/11/2008 and merged with Ner Zedek-Ezrath Israel-Beth Uziel
[Hungarian]

Started as trade Shul and beneficial society. First met in a house at 4th & Lombard Sts. Moved in 1892 to 322 Bainbridge St. and served the 4th Street business district. Moved in 1909 to the S.4th St. Rented and then purchased the synagogue in 1917. A craft community with interior built by members. Meeting place for several local beneficial societies. Combined with Mishkan Israel & Raim Ahuvim Anshe Kalker in 1961 to become Shivtei Yeshurun at 4th & Mercy Sts. In 1968 combined with Heisner & Ezras Israel, and then with synagogues at 6th & Sigel, 6th & Morris, 4th & Morris, 5th & Mercy, 5th & Dudley and 4th & Porter.
Began as Yiddish speaking B'nai Israel in Chester, PA in 1891; combined with another synagogue in 1920. Built Shul in 1927 and became Conservative. Moved to present location in 1965.

Rented space at American & Porter Sts. Synagogue built in 1920. Chevra Tehilim & Gemilas organized in 1935 with money donated "in memoriam." Eventually name & funds of the congregation were transferred to Emes V'Zedek in Beth Shean in Israel.
Ladies Hebrew Sunday School Society of Frankford organized 1906. Became Adath Zion.
Services held in home across street from Mount Sinai Hospital (Daroff Southern Division of Albert Einstein). Conducted by a melamed, Max Jaffe. After his death in the 1930's the minyan dissolved.
Started as a Landsleit Shul.
Met in homes until 1926. Opened new synagogue in Strawberry Mansion at 32nd & Page Sts. Monastricher Rebbe briefly used Shul.

Used hotel space on Parkside Ave until construction of Synagogue on same site. Had Chevra Mishnais (study group); Ladies' Auxiliary (1924) & Sisterhood (1940's).Synagogue built & dedicated in 1926; combined with Beth El Suburban in Broomall in 1972. Subsequently, Christ Community Baptist Church. Memorial plaques remain in Church.

Members came from two towns separated by the Dniester River. Held services in German and Hebrew. Shared space with Yeshiva Mishkan Israel. [Ataki (Bessarabia) & Moghilev Podolski, NW of Odessa (Ukraine)]
Served the businesses along 48th & Spruce. Became a Jewish center in 1965 for kashrut, religious psychiatry, ritual and advice.
Served businessmen on 1500 block of S. 7th St.
Began as a Chevra Mishnais. Purchased home at 7525 Loretta Ave as synagogue in 1979. Moved to Rhawn St.
Started as a Landsleit Shul.
Combined with Agudas Achim Rumanian Congregation in 1892. [Rumanian]

Oldest active Shul in Phl. Started in a small house in Sterling Alley, west of 3rd St. Moved in 1776 to Cherry Alley between 3rd and 4th Sts., a member's home. Dedicated a Shul on Cherry Street in 1782. Rebuilt and rededicated Shul at same location in 1825. Erected new building at 7th above Arch in 1860; moved to Broad and York in 1909 and to current location in 1976.

Bustleton Congregation formed in 1955. First met in people's homes and became Conservative. Purchased land at 9200 Bustleton Ave. Held services in tent until construction was done. Synagogue dedicated in 1963 as Temple Beth Am. For several years employed a blind cantor.
Services held at 724 Snyder until dedication of new building in 1929. Records held by Rabbi Rabinowitz and Sara Feldman (316 Albenis St).

Served businesses on 52nd St. Had Sunday School and Sisterhood in 1930's & 1940's. First met in rented space north of Market St, & later at 5236 Chestnut. In the 1920's a portion of the community broke away. Older members remained and renamed the congregation Beth Judah. Subsequently, Enon Chapel Baptist Church.
Served commercial district at Allegheny & Kensington. Became Conservative after WWII.
Began in private houses. Previous location at 506 E. Erie (at Franklinville). Mortgage paid in 1940's.
Started as a Landsleit Shul.
Jewish Community Center started in store front at 3226 W. Cheltenham Ave until building of Center in late 1950's. Combined with Beth Sholom of Elkins Park
Served the Hebrew sheltering homes in vicinity.
Former members of B'nai Menasha.
Served N. 22nd St. commercial district. [Refugee German]
In 1969 B'nai Aaron of Wynnefield merged. In 1991 Yeadon Jewish Community Center joined the merger.

Founded as Young Israel of Wynnefield by returning veterans from the Korean War. Rented a house on N.51st. Purchased a mansion at 5301 Wynnefield Ave in 1963. Directors were Samuel Back; Hilton H Atlas; Murry Lefkowitz; Simon Felder; Meyer Schuster; Alter Diament and Gilbert I Yaros. Moved to Main line and changed name in 1996.

Started as a Landsleit Shul known as B'nai Israel or the Hollander Shul. Met in homes until purchase of building. Refurbished in 1926. Combined with Krakauer B.A. in 1879 to become, Krakauer-Beth Elohim.
A shtiebl shul. President, Ephraim Savodovsky

First rented rooms in the building later purchased. 2029 S. 6th also purchased for use by sexton in 1918. Combined with the Fastover Shul at 6th & Sigel in 1965. Subsequently New Life Evangelistic church. Records held by Raymond Rudman (2413 S Sheridan St).
Started as a Landsleit Shul and beneficial association.
Services held in rented rooms on SE corner of 4th & South until 1902 purchase of synagogue building. Combined in 1903 with Prushzvier Shershow.
Started in Silver family home. Site at Verree off Welsh Rds. Adopted name of Congregation Shaare Shomayim from South Philly
Started as Landsleit Shul. A Chasidic congregation. Members were all newly arrived immigrants in 1920's. Moved to Strawberry Mansion in 1930's. See, Chevra Vohliner Anshe Kuppel. [Vohlyn Gubernia]

Successor to the Kensington & Allegheny synagogue organized by Harry Weiss. Met at 1100 Oak Lane Rd. After a fire, rented space at 67th & Lakeside; then held services at the Cedarbrook County Club in Wyncote. Disbanded when Rabbi Levine died.

Chartered as B'nai Aruyh Moshel. Rechartered under present name in 1916. Services held in member homes until 1910 when a two storey brown brick Shul was dedicated. Remodeled 1915. No building or other dues required of new members.
Started as a house Shul. Built new Synagogue/ Community Center in 1949/50. In late 1960's the synagogue combined with the Suburban Jewish Community Center in Havertown.
Community followed locale rabbinate.

Started as a Landsleit Shul. Two Rumanian congregations formed in 1905, Poras Joseph and Agudas Achim. Latter rented space at 512 S. 3rd (at Gaskill), and in 1910 moved to larger quarters at 4th & Spruce Sts. In 1911 took over Baptist Church at 426 Spruce St. and was renamed Beth HaMedrash HaGodol Anshe
Cong of manual workers and small shopkeepers in 7th & Tasker area. Broke away from Anshe Zitomer. Adopted new name, Aitz Chaim, in 1930.
Broke from the Heisner Shul and build a large synagogue at 8th & Porter Sts, named Shari Eli.

First name was Logan Congregation Ahavas Israel in rented space in a Theatre. At time of charter, was Conservative. Changed its name to Beth Sholom in honor of the Armistice of 1918. Hired a rabbi, Mortimer Cohen, and built a synagogue at Broad, Courland & Belfield, dedicated in 1922 (Italianate style). In the late 40's work began on a new community center and school in Elkins Park. The new synagogue, designed by Frank Lloyd Wright was dedicated Sept 1959. Krainin; Aaron Landes

Started as a Landsleit Shul. First Austro-Hungarian cong in South Phl. Served 4th & Bainbridge Sts business district. By 1907 met at 343 Monroe; 10 years later met at 322 Bainbridge. In 1929 purchased a private home at 59th & Cedar and remodeled as Shul. Records held by Alex Zlotnick (5813 Pine St). Moved to Wynnefield around 1959. [Austro-Hungarian]
In a housing area, this Conservative Shul chose path between the Orthodox Beth Emeth and the Reform congregations to the west of Roosevelt Blvd.
Founded in Doris & Leo Levin's home. In 1956 a mansion on the current site was purchased and in 1980 a new Shul was built behind the mansion. In 2002 the mansion was demolished and the Shul enlarged.
Founded by Joseph Asbell, a custom tailor, to provide a neighborhood synagogue for his orthodox friends.
Met at 615 N. 2nd St. (1903-1906). Rented hall space for High Holidays on NE corner of 3rd & Brown Sts. until purchase of N. 4th St. building in 1906. [Lithuanian]
Beneficial association and Landsleit Shul. [Postiver]
At site of the Uptown Ladies' Home for the Aged. Previously [the Home/Moshav Zekenim] located at 720 N. American St. ('13-15), 957 N. Finch St. (1915-23) and 933 Fairmount Ave (1923-4).

Started as a Landsleit Shul. Synagogue of the Monastreishner Rebbe, Mayer Isaacson. Moved to Mount Airy in 1961. 6th & Wolf, three storey red brick building erected in 1922, sold to Anshe Zitomer in the 70's. [Rumanian]
Changed name several times: Toras Israel in 1922; Bialick Cong - after chazan, Jacob Bialick - in 1933, and Beth Zion in 1937.

Started as a Landsleit Shul in rented space at 1717 S. 8th then at 5th & Greenwich in 1916. The cong split in 1916, younger group remained on S. 8th as The Hesiner Independent Young Men's Society and the older congregants continued at 5th & Greenwich and later 7th & Morris. In 1920 purchased a single storey red brick building on S.6th St as a Shul. The Heisner Rebbe was related to the Monastreichner Rebbe. Sexton quarters created in 1929. Records held by Joseph Goodman (2717 W Girard Ave) and Rose Grossman (1418 S 5th St). [Hesin]

Yellow brick building built in 1904. Altered in 1938. Wall murals by Morris Balk, workshop at 511 South St. Also hired for murals at B'nai Abraham (5th & Lombard), Kesher Israel (4th & Lombard), Anshe Sode Loven (5th & Mercy), Beth Samuel (Marshall & Ritner). Records held by Philip Ravitch (1507 S 4th St) and Freda Moliva (1709 S 5th St). [Austro-Hungarian]
Services and night classes directed by the German-Jewish organizers of Touro Hall. Provided free High Holiday services.
Trade Shul of shochtim (ritual slaughterers) under Rabbi Ephraim Yolles. Relocated from Strawberry Mansion.
Italianate style built 1928.
Members were residents of the Hebrew Sheltering Home. When the Home closed in 1924, the name was transferred to a new synagogue in West Philadelphia. 3 storey red brick purchased, 1911.
Started as a Landsleit Shul.
Construction took 15 years: 1951-64. Disbanded by 1982 and combined with Temple Sinai in 1984.

Hebrew Free Burial Society organized by Rabbi Berel Weisman in 1898. First met in private homes. Purchased building at 415 N. 4th in 1901. Temporary move to 730 Poplar St in 1934. 415 N. 4th destroyed by fire in 1937.
Disbanded as members moved to Logan. In late 1960's Rabbi Lipschitz moved his Shul to Flatbush, Brooklyn

Temporarily at 76th Ave & Limekiln Pike, then met at a food market at Ogontz Ave. & Washington Lane until purchase of site at the Pennypacker school in the mid-40's. Twenty years later combined with the Suburban Temple of Montgomery County. Dedicated current Shul in 1971.
Served commercial district at 52nd & Market.
Constructed in 1959.

Started as a Landsleit Shul. Services in rented rooms until 1915, then at NE corner of 3rd & Lombard Sts for one year until purchase of building on Pine. Women members organized the Sara Shapiro Sisterhood. Anshe Zitomer joined with Anshe Vilna in early 1970's. [Vilna]
In 1940 moved and became Brith Sholom Community Center. Synagogue housed in the Paschall- Garrick House ("Old Yellow Mansion") built 1723. Records held by Benjamin Gross and Jacob Rossikov.
Student led classes and services.

Started as a Landsleit Shul. AKA as Anshe Jaffe after the Jaffe family, major contributors to the synagogue and its societies. Catered mainly to the local businessmen who worked Saturdays. Pre 1897 services held above a store on the NE corner 7th & Bainbridge. Remodeled from a 3- storey red brick home to Byzantine style in 1897, 1904 and 1914 after a fire. Some old records held by Morris Sheidan 743 Morris St.
[Sephardic]
Daily services were held on the grounds of the hospital

In 1886 Poale Zedek held services at NW corner of 6th & South Sts, then at 518 South St. (1889). In 1890 the newly merged congregation Poale Zedek Anshe Birz met at 518 S. St, then at 322 Bainbridge St. In 1908 Poale Zedek-Sharith Israel dedicated a new synagogue on S. 5th Sts. Three smaller congregations combined in 1908 - Chevra B'nai Joseph, Chevra Shearith Israel & Chevra Beth Jacob Anshe Libowitz - to form, Poale Zedek Sharith Israel. Combined with B'nai Moishe at 5th & Watkin in early 1960's. [Birz & Yanishkel, Kovno]
Site purchased in 1951, building completed by 1959.

Served businessmen along Woodland Ave. Former Island Road members migrated to here in the 1910's. Met in homes until 1926. Younger generation founded own congregation, Beth Am. Subsequently, New Home Mission Baptist Church. [Hungarian]

First met at 3118 W. Columbia Ave. The largest Orthodox cong in vicinity. Rabbi Ephraim Yolles headed the local organization of orthodox synagogues. 3 storey, gray brick Romanesque built in 1896 as church; remodeled as synagogue in 1914. Records held by Hyman Metzger (1938 N 32nd St).
Became Beth David in late 1930's. Named after the Apter Rebbe, David Hershel. Former members of Kerem Israel.
Started as a Landsleit Shul.
Served the business community along Girard (at 26th -30th.). Some members had broken from Tifereth Israel at 3940 Girard to form Shearith Israel. [Mix of Hungarian & Russian]

First met at B'nai Israel, Castor & Solly Aves., then at the Rhawnhurst Jewish Community Center. In the early 80's acquired a home at 9900 Verree Road (near Red Lion Rd) and renamed, Beit Harambam -The Northeast Sephardic Congregation. [Israeli Sephardim]

Trade Shul of master carpenters known as The Carpenters' Shul Combined with Shivtei Yeshurun (of 2014 S. 4th) in 1962; became Ezras Israel 3 row home containing the synagogue and a Talmud Torah. Central Bimah. Ladies' Balcony on 2nd floor. Use of embossed brick with donor names on building exterior. Subsequent use by an African-American church.
Minyan held at home of the Harris family - Hebrew speakers from Odessa - and called the Yavne Synagogue. Synagogue closed on Chaim Harris' death in 1970.
Services in private homes until Poplar St. was rebuilt as Shul in 1921. Beth Jacob combined with Tifereth Israel in 1962. Records held by Mr. Pearlstein (1113 N 41st St).
Earlier location at 2nd & Vine. Merged with B'nai Jacob in 1962.
Named in honor of founding benefactor, Morris Abrams. Originally called B'nai Moishe, and nicknamed, The White Shul. Two adjacent brownstones; subsequently, Redeem Baptist Church. [White Russian]
